findings.description: Client-controlled session.filename is joined into UPLOAD_DIR with no sanitization, allowing path traversal or absolute-path writes to arbitrary filesystem locations.
assemble iterates range(total_chunks - 1), skipping the final chunk so every reassembled file is truncated and corrupt.
assemble commits the file to UPLOAD_DIR but never calls verify_checksum or file_size_ok, so integrity and size are never enforced before storage.
verify_checksum reads the entire file into memory via f.read() (up to 200MB+ and size is unenforced), enabling memory exhaustion under load.
No per-chunk or aggregate size limit and total_chunks is unbounded, so MAX_FILE_MB is never enforced and an attacker can exhaust disk space.
upload_id is interpolated into the chunk filesystem path without validation, allowing path injection via crafted upload_id values.
Two concurrent sessions with the same filename overwrite each other; writes are non-atomic and the _sessions dict is accessed without locking.
File I/O (open/write/copyfileobj) has no exception handling, so disk-full, permission, or missing-chunk errors propagate uncaught and leave partial files and orphaned state.
Extension check passes degenerate filenames such as a bare '.pdf' or empty path components.
